coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots based on arginine and maleic acid for fabrication of PVA composite films and iron fluorescence probes
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Huang
Tang, Yuran
Zhu, He
Xiao, Man
Guo, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have a wide application prospect in biomedical, fluorescent probes and other fields due to their abundant advantages, such as excellent optical properties, low toxicity, etc... In this essay, carbon dots with nitrogen-doped (MA-CDs) carrying good biocompatibility and relatively high absolute fluorescence quantum yield (12.37%) was prepared by microwave synthesis using maleic acid with good conjugated structure as carbon precursor and arginine with the highest content of nitrogen in amino acids as nitrogen source. The UV-vis absorption spectra and time-resolved spectra show that it has dual light centers, and the luminescence center 2 has a long fluorescence lifetime (25.7602 ns). In addition, MA-CDs has excellent water solubility, good photobleaching resistance, and good stability in physiological environment. Based on this, we successfully prepared MA-CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite films and constructed iron ion fluorescence probes with wide linear range.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon dot
Composite film
Microwave synthesis
Fluorescent probe
Iron

Preparation of PVA film composited with MA-CDs
Fabrication of fluorescence probe for Fe3+
The selectivity of MA-CDs to Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of different concentrations of MA-CDs at different λexs.
Fluorescence emission spectra and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of MA-CDs in different solvents under excitation at 290 nm.
UV absorption spectra of MA-CDs and their fluorescence emission under 290 nm excitation.
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of A-CDs and M-CDs.

Waterfall diagram of MA-CDs at different pH under excitation at 290 nm (A), emission spectra at different pH under excitation at 290 nm (B), Waterfall diagram of MA-CDs under excitation at 290 nm in different concentrations of NaCl solution (C), light stability of MA-CDs under continuous 2 h light irradiation (D).
TEM image of MA-CDs (A) (B); particle size distribution of MA-CDs (C).
Full spectrum of XPS of MA-CDs (A), C1s high-resolution spectrum (B), N1s high-resolution spectrum (C), O1s high-resolution spectrum (D).
Cell viability changes when hela cells are co-cultured with different concentrations of MA-CDs for 24 h.
Fluorescence emission spectrum of the film under excitation at 365 nm (A); UV absorption spectrum of the film (B).
Fluorescence effect of the film under excitation at 365 nm (MA-CDs/PVA film (top), raw PVA film (bottom)).
The influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of MA-CDs.
Linear relationship between iron ion concentration and MA-CDs (left); emission spectrum of different iron ion concentration.
Illustration of the synthesis of MA-CDs and schematic diagram of quenching
Fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield of MA-CDs under Ex of 290 nm.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (λex: Ex; λem: Em; τ(ns): fluorescence lifetime/decay index; Rel: proportion of decay index; τaverage: average fluorescence lifetime; χ2: degree of fit).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The percentage of C, N, O of MA-CDs.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots based on arginine and maleic acid for fabrication of PVA composite films and iron fluorescence probes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Institute of Chemical Industry, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Institute of Chemical Industry, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Institute of Chemical Industry, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have a wide application prospect in biomedical, fluorescent probes and other fields due to their abundant advantages, such as excellent optical properties, low toxicity, etc... In this essay, carbon dots with nitrogen-doped (MA-CDs) carrying good biocompatibility and relatively high absolute fluorescence quantum yield (12.37%) was prepared by microwave synthesis using maleic acid with good conjugated structure as carbon precursor and arginine with the highest content of nitrogen in amino acids as nitrogen source. The UV-vis absorption spectra and time-resolved spectra show that it has dual light centers, and the luminescence center 2 has a long fluorescence lifetime (25.7602 ns). In addition, MA-CDs has excellent water solubility, good photobleaching resistance, and good stability in physiological environment. Based on this, we successfully prepared MA-CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite films and constructed iron ion fluorescence probes with wide linear range.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots MA-CDs with good biocompatibility and high absolute fluorescence quantum yield (12.37%) was prepared by microwave synthesis using maleic acid with good conjugated structure as carbon source and arginine with the highest nitrogen content in amino acids as nitrogen source. In addition, MA-CDs has excellent water solubility, good photobleaching resistance, and good stability in physiological environment. Based on this, we successfully prepared MA-CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite films and constructed iron ion fluorescence probes with wide linear range.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an important trace element for maintaining human health and an essential part of hemoglobin, cytochrome enzyme and catalase [1]. Iron deficiency in the human body can cause iron-deficiency anemia, which can lead to decreased motor function, decreased intelligence, decreased immunity, etc.; when the body consumes excessive iron, it can cause iron poisoning and increase the risk of heart disease [2]. Therefore, neither low nor high ingestion of iron inside human blood is conducive to health, even life-threatening in severe cases. Therefore, the determination of the content of ions has extremely important practical significance [3–5].

Carbon dots (CDs) are defined as small scattered spherical nanoparticles (below 10 nm) that can absorb excitation light to produce fluorescent [6]. CDs not only have luminescence properties and nano-size characteristics similar to traditional quantum dots, but also have good water solubility, easy functionalized, high photobleaching resistance, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. It has broad application prospects in various fields such as medical imaging technology, environmental monitoring, chemical analysis, etc. [7–13].

CDs mainly contain three parts: carbon core, surface functional groups and edge functional groups. The carbon core is mainly derived from a carbon source and has a C=C conjugated structure, while the surface and edge functional group is mainly derived from a precursor or a chain functional group of the solvent [9,14]. Amino acids are rich in carboxyl and amino functional groups and are one of the ideal carbon sources for preparing CDs with excellent biocompatibility [15,16]. Due to its highest nitrogen content within 20 essential amino acids, arginine is often used as a carbon source by scientific researchers to prepare unique carbon dots. For example, Arcudi et al. used arginine and ethylenediamine as precursor to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) with a small size and high quantum yield (QY) [17]. Huang et al. synthesized CDs with high crystallinity using arginine as a carbon source and glycerin as a surface passivator and applied them in electrocatalysis [16].

Herein, maleic acid with a C=C structure and arginine were used for precursors to prepare N-CDs (MA-CDs) using uniformly heated and rapid microwave synthesis method. In the synthesis process, maleic acid act as carbon source, and arginine play for nitrogen source. Under high temperature and high pressure, after precursors polycondensated and carbonizated, MA-CDs formed, which are rich in amino, hydroxyl, epoxide, carbonyl on the surface. It has a relatively high absolute fluorescence quantum yield, about 12.37%. The luminescence mechanism through UV-vis absorption spectroscopy and time-resolved spectroscopy was studied and the results indicate that the MA-CDs have dual emission centers, one of which has a longer fluorescence lifetime (25.7602 ns). Meanwhile, the MA-CDs exibited a high solubility in water. When the concentration is as high as 5 mg/mL, its fluorescence intensity still does not decrease significantly. In addition, the MA-CDs have good photobleaching resistance, stability, and good stability in the range of pH=3–9, indicating that the MA-CDs has good stability under physiological conditions and has biological application prospects. According to its great stability, the MA-CDs/PVA composite film was successfully prepared in this paper. The abundant groups hanging on the surface of CDs can chelate with Fe3+ to quench their fluorescence [18]. Based on this, this paper successfully constructed a Fe3+ fluorescent probe with high sensitivity and wide linear range Scheme 1 .

Microwave synthesis reactor (Monowave 400, Anton Paar, Austria), Vacuum drying oven(Yiheng Scientific Instruments, China), Rotary evaporator (RE-52AA, Yarong Biochemical, China), Attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (ATR-FTIR) Spectrometer Frontier, Platinum Elmer, American), Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEM 2100F, JEOL, Janpan), Steady State Transient Fluorescence Spectrometer (FLS1000/FS5, Edinburgh, UK), Fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer (Duetta, Horiba, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), CO2 constant temperature incubator (Append China Co., Ltd., model: Galaxy 170 S), Ultra-clean workbench (Shanghai Dute Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., model: SW-CJ-2FD), Enzyme label Instrument (TECAN, model: SPARK 10 M).

L-Arginine (Arg), Maleic acid (MA), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (average MW 31,000–50,000), NaOH, Hydrochloric acid, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·3H2O, Cu(NO3)2· 3H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, CoCl2, ZnSO4, NaCl, KBr, and LiBr are purchased from Shanghai Macklin Bioreagent Co., Ltd. Calf serum (Gibco, article number: 10,270-106), DMEM high glucose medium (Corning, article number: 10-013-CVRC), PBS (Gibco, article number: C10010500BT), pancreatin (Biosharp, article number: 143,188), double Anti-(Hyclone, article number SV30010), DMSO (Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.), MTT (Solarbio). All experiments used self-made ultrapure water by distilled water machine.

Preparation of MA-CDs: 250 mg Arg and 50 mg MA were added into a 30 mL microwave reaction flask, and at the same time 10 mL ultrapure water was pipetted. Then, the mixture was put into microwave synthesis reactor and reacted at 220 °C for 2 h, with a stirring speed of 600 rpm. The original solution gradually changed from colorless transparent solution to yellow-brown transparent solution. After cooling to 55 °C, the solution was taken out, and then the resulting solution was concentrated with a rotary evaporator at 70 °C. After freeze-drying, a yellow-brown solid was obtained, which was sealed by sealing film and stored in a desiccator for later use.

Preparation of MA-CDs/PVA: 2 g PVA was weighed into dry beakers A and B, respectively, and then 10 mL ultrapure water was added. The solutions were stirred magnetically at 90 °C for half an hour until clear and transparent. 3 mL 20 mg/mL MA-CDs was injected into beaker A to form solution A. The solution A was stirred for 1 h at 90 °C until uniform. After cooling completely, 2 ml solution A and B were dropwised to the bottom of the petri dish to cover it up, respectively. Next, two dishes were dried at 70 °C. Finally, MA-CDs/PVA film and blank PVA film are obtained.

TEM was used for observing the morphological characteristics and lattice spacing of MA-CDs; collection of the surface functional groups of MA-CDs was performed on ATR-FTIR spectroscopy and XPS. The prepared MA-CDs powder was made into a 1 mg/mL solution, and fluorescence emission spectra (FL) and UV-vis-visible absorption spectra (UV-vis) were collected to study its optical properties. At the same time, the MTT method was carried out to check out the cytotoxicity of MA-CDs to study the biocompatibility. QY and fluorescence lifetime (τ) were collected on steady state transient fluorescence spectrometer.

To explore releated luminescence performance of MA-CDs, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 mg/mL MA-CDs solution was prepared, and their fluorescence emission spectrum was collected. As shown in Fig. 1 , MA-CDs always exhibit dual emission characteristics with different concentration, which has two fluorescence emission centers in UV-vis and visible blue regions, respectively. Among them, under the excitation wavelength (λex) of 280 nm–300 nm, the UV-vis emission region around 350 nm has no obvious λex dependence. The luminous intensity generally increases with the increase of concentration, but there is local fluctuation, which may be due to the increase of concentration, the self-absorption phenomenon caused by the luminescence in the blue region absorbs the fluorescence in the UV-vis region. Once the λex increases from 300 nm to 420 nm, the visible blue region gradually becomes prominent. As the concentration increasing, the emission intensity gradually increases, and as the λex increasing, the emission wavelength (λem) has a significant red shift, indicating that the MA-CDs has excellent water solubility.

To explore the influence of solvent polarity on the optical properties, MA-CDs were dissolved in ultrapure water, DMSO, MeOH, Acetone with different polarities, and fluorescence spectra were collected. As shown in Fig. 2 , the emission of the UV-vis region and the blue region exhibit different degrees of blue shift and red shift in different solvents. As the solvent polarity decreases, the emission position in the UV-vis region does not change significantly, but the intensity gradually decreases, while the emission in the blue region increases as the solvent polarity decreases, and the λem gradually red shifts, which also shows that the two parts of the emission have different sources [19].

To explore the luminescence mechanism of MA-CDs, UV absorption spectra were collected for MA-CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that MA-CDs have broadband absorption in the range of 250 nm–310 nm (π-π* transition of sp2), corresponding to the excitation in the UV-vis region, indicating that the UV-vis regional launches mainly come from carbon nuclei. Broadband absorption around 310 nm–500 nm is formed by the n-π* transition of C=N bonds or C=O bonds on surface, which corresponds to the excitation of the blue emission region of MA-CDs, indicating that the emission of the blue region is mainly from depression in the surface region [20]. MA and Arg bring multiple groups to MA-CDs surface, which have different surface state levels, and have many different emission traps on the surface, so that they have different emission wavelengths under different λex [21].

To further verify the above inference, the fluorescence lifetime data of MA-CDs solution was collected at an λex of 290 nm. According to the analysis of the spectrum in Fig. 4 and the data in Table 1 , it's easy to speculate MA-CDs have two luminescence centers (1, 2), which corresponds to the results of the above-mentioned UV-vis characterization data, and the fluorescence lifetimes τ1 and τ2 of the two luminescence centers (1, 2) are 3.8694 ns and 25.7602 ns, respectively, accounting for 73.96 and 26.04% respectively, and the average fluorescence lifetime is 9.5706 ns. Under the excitation of 290 nm wavelength, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of MA-CDs is 12.37%. Based on the results of the above characterization data, it is speculated that MA-CDs have two luminescence centers, of which τ=3.8694 ns is the main luminescence center [22].

To further study the source of luminescence, we separately performed microwave reaction on Arg and MA and measured their fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5 ). M-CDs emit both in the UV-vis region around 350 nm and the blue region, which indicates that the emission around 350 nm in the UV region of MA-CDs mainly comes from maleic acid. A-CDs mainly emit in visible blue region. It can be inferred that this emission comes from the synergy of arginine and MA, which comes from the abundant surface functional groups of Arg and MA. Surface groups hanging on CDs formed surface defects [19].

MA-CDs were placed in different NaCl concentrations and different pH solutions configured by HCl and NaOH to observe its stability under λex of 290 nm. From Fig. 6 (A), the emission intensity in the pH range of 3–9 has a small change, but gradually decreases when the pH is 10–12, which indicates that MA-CDs can be used in organisms. As shown in Fig. 6(B), when the pH is 1, 2 or 9–13, there is a significant red shift. In the solution with a pH of 3–8, the λem does not change much. It is speculated that, in strong acidic or alkaline solution, the functional groups on the surface of MA-CDs may be protonated or deprotonated. Seen from Fig. 6(C), fluorescence intensity has few changes in the solution with 0–2.0 mol/L NaCl, indicating that the salt solution concentration does not have much effect on it. To explore its light stability, MA-CDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was light irradiated for 2 h and the fluorescence intensity was detected. As shown in Fig. 6(D), the emission intensity did not change significantly, indicating MA-CDs own good light stability [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of MA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images show that MA-CDs are spherical, with good dispersion and with agglomeration. The particle size is roughly distributed between 7 and 13 nm, and the average particle size is about 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 7 (C).

The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 8 (A)) and Table 2 show that the MA-CDs contains three elements: C (285.32 eV, C1s), N (400.44 eV, N1s), and O (531.56 eV, O1s), which fully demonstrates the success of the N atom Doped and modified into MA-CDs. The C1s (Fig. 8(B)) shows typical peaks of 284.84 eV (C=C/C-C), 286.54 eV(C-O-C), 288.62 eV(O-C=C/-CONH-) and 291.55 eV(C=O. The N1s ((Fig. 8(C))) shows three peaks at 399.91 eV, 401.65 eV and 403.25 eV. The peak at 399.91 eV corresponds to the amide nitrogen atom and the peak at 401.65 eV corresponds to the pyrrole nitrogen. And the peak at 403.25 eV corresponds to the amino group. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 8(D)) shows that O1s is decomposed into two peaks, the C-O peak at 532.02 eV and the O-H bond at 535.11 eV [24]. The results show that the surface of MA-CDs has abundant oxygen-containing functional groups [15,17,25].

To further explore the structure of MA-CDs, they were characterized by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 9 ). The peaks located at 3203 cm−1 (O-H),2944 cm−1 and 2873 cm−1(C-H) , 1640 cm−1(-C=O/C=C/N-H) ,1492 cm−1 and 1388 cm−1(pyridine ring) and 1348 cm−1(C-N) are consistent with the results of XPS. Based on the above analysis, the surface of MA-CDs contains abundant groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl [23].

According to the above structural characterization results, combined with literature, MA undergoes dehydration in a high temperature and high-pressure environment, further cross-linking and carbonization to form carbon nucleus; arginine is used as a nitrogen source, and nitrogen is decomposed and doped or modified to the surface of carbon nucleus or carbon dots under high temperature and pressure to form MA-CDs rich in surface functional groups [8,9,26].

To explore the biocompatibility of MA-CDs, the MTT method was used to detect the changes in cytoactive of Hela cells with MA-CDs co-cultured for 24 h. As the concentration of MA-CDs increases, the 24 h cell viability gradually decreases, but the decrease is smaller (Fig. 10 ). After co-cultured with 500 μg/mL MA-CDs, the cell survival rate is still more than 90%, which fully shows that MA-CDs have good biocompatibility and have good biological application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA film composited with MA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent light stability of MA-CDs, a PVA film doped with MA-CDs (MA-CDs/PVA film) was successfully prepared. To collected the fluoresence properties of PVA/MA-CDs films, raw material PVA films and MA-CDs/PVA films were characterized by fluorescence and UV-vis absorption, respectively. It can be observed from Fig. 11 the emission range and intensity of the MA-CDs/PVA film is significantly broader and higher than that of the blank PVA film. At the same time, compared with PVA film, the UV absorption of the MA-CDs/PVA film has new absorption peak derived from MA-CDs. It shows that MA-CDs is successfully doped into PVA film, and its fluorescence mainly depends on MA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 12 , the MA-CDs/PVA film (top) emits obvious blue fluorescence under excitation of 365 nm, while the PVA film has no obvious phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescence probe for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of MA-CDs has abundant oxygen-containing groups. Transition metal ions can complex with these oxygen-containing groups to form chelate. The electrons in the excited MA-CDs will transfer to Fe3+, causing the electrons to undergo a non-radiative transition, which will cause the fluorescence quenching of MA-CDs. Therefore MA-CDs can be used to construct fluorescent probes for detecting metal ions [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of MA-CDs to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the feasibility of MA-CDs for metal ion detection, take 100 μL of 5 mmol/L different metal salt solution and 100 μL of 20 mmol/L MA-CDs solution, then add 1800 μL of ultrapure water to it, and measured its fluorescence emission spectrum under 290 nm excitation. Different metal ions on the MA-CDs (excitation at 290 nm) the influence of the emission intensity as charted in Fig. 13 . Compared to Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+ or Li+ for MA-CDs fluorescence quenching, iron ion exhibits best. Based on this, this article will use MA-CDs to construct fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.

200 μL 10 mg/mL MA-CDs solution was injected into 1.7 mL ultrapure water. Then 100 uL 10 mmol iron ions was added. Then, the fluorescence intensity under excitation at 290 nm wavelength were measured after 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 40 min, 50 min, and 60 min (Fig. 14 ). The fluorescence intensity of the system is the smallest when the reaction time is 10 min, and the fluorescence intensity does not fluctuate too much during the 20–60 min process, so 10 min is selected as the final incubation time of the fluorescent probe.

200 uL 10 mg/L MA-CDs solution was added into 1.7 mL of ultrapure water, then 100 uL of metal ions of different concentrations (0.01–100 μmol·L−1) were added and incubated for 10 min. Then the fluorescence intensity at 290 nm excitation and 350 nm λemwas measured. It can be seen from Fig. 15 that when the concentration of Fe3+ in the solution is in the range of 0.01–100 μmol·L−1, the emission intensity changes linearly with the increase of concentration of Fe3+, which are summarized as the linear regression equation (c: μmol·L−1):
F = − 26.33758 c + 6683.06701
(
R 2
= 0.993
)

In this paper, with maleic acid and L-arginine as raw materials, MA-CDs with an absolute fluorescence QY of 12.37% were synthesized in microwave reactor, and the related properties of MA-CDs were studied. The characterization results state that average diameter of the MA-CDs is about 10 nm, have excellent water solubility, and have dual emission centers, one of which has a fluorescence lifetime of 25.7602 ns. In addition, the MA-CDs has stable fluorescence performance under physiological conditions, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility. More importantly, MA-CDs/PVA films with blue light emission were prepared Based on the good photobleaching resistance of the MA-CDs. At the same time, based on MA-CDs with good selectivity to Fe3+, a fluorescent probe with a wide linear range was successfully constructed.

Huang Tang: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuran Tang: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision. He Zhu: Methodology. Man Xiao: Methodology. Mingming Guo: Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest relevant to this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the support of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities: Southwest University (NO. 5330500110, NO. 5240101332), National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (NO. 202110635101), Provincial Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (NO. 7110600001/184).
